draft.introduction: The opening of Deutero-Isaiah, announcing comfort and restoration after exile. “A voice crying in the wilderness” was applied to John the Baptist by all four Gospels.

translation.text: Comfort, comfort my people, says your God. Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and cry to her that her warfare is ended. A voice cries: “In the wilderness prepare the way of the Lord; make straight in the desert a highway for our God.”
translation.original_text: παρακαλεῖτε παρακαλεῖτε ὁ λαός μου λέγει ὁ θεός

translation.text: Have you not known? Have you not heard? The Lord is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He does not faint or grow weary. They who wait for the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings like eagles.
translation.original_text: οἱ δὲ ὑπομένοντες τὸν θεὸν ἀλλάξουσιν ἰσχύν

translator_notes.note: The LXX places the phrase “in the wilderness” with “prepare” rather than with “a voice crying,” yielding “a voice crying: In the wilderness prepare.” The Gospels follow the LXX punctuation, reading it as “a voice crying in the wilderness.”
